coredata.dc:title: Visual detection of H2O2 and glucose by HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Jie
Jiang, Han
Wu, Yiwei
Yu, Xiaoxiao
coredata.dc:description: This study presents the development of a sensitive and simple enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform in the consist of CdTe quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), and hepatitis B core antibody labeled with horseradish peroxidase (HBcAb-HRP) for the visual analysis of H2O2 and glucose. The sulfur atoms in HBcAb-HRP have a strong affinity for Cd(II), which effectively enhances the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs due to the generation of more radiative centers at the CdTe/Cd-SR complex. In the presence of H2O2, the Cd-S bonds are oxidized to form disulfide products and results in linear fluorescence quenching, while CDs maintain stable. Becasue glucose can be converted into H2O2 with the aid of glucose oxidase, this sensing platform can also be used for analyzing glucose. The detection limits for H2O2 and glucose are 2.9 μmol L−1 with RSD of 2.6% and 1.6 μmol L−1 with RSD of 2.4% respectively. In addition, under UV lamp irradiation, the orange-yellow CdTe QDs gradually quench with increasing H2O2 and glucose, while the blue CDs remain unchanged. A color change from orange-yellow to blue enables a visual semi-quantitative determination of H2O2 in commercial contact lens solution and glucose in human serum without any pretreatment. Thus, this CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric sensing platform has significant potential for the rapid analysis of H2O2 and glucose in actual application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
H2O2
Glucose
Human serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs and CDs
Construction of CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP for the detection of H2O2 and glucose
Visual detection of H2O2 and glucose in actual samples
Principles for visual assay H2O2 and glucose by CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP
Composition of HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs
Effect of reaction time on the detection of H2O2
Effect of GOx dosage and reaction time on the detection of glucose

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology of Hubei Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China. [2022CFD038].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of CdTe QDs, TGA and CDs. (b) UV-absorption of CdTe QDs, CDs and HBcAb-HRP. (c) Zeta potential of CdTe QDs, CDs, CdTe QDs/CDs, CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP, CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP+CDs. (d). Fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs, CDs, HBcAb-HRP, CdTe QDs/CDs, CdTe QDs/HBcAb-HRP, CDs/HBcAb-HRP and CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP at the same concentration.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform in the present of different concentration of H2O2 (9.00–500 μmol L−1). (b) The plot of I 440/I 570 versus the corresponding concentrations of H2O2. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/ CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform in the present of different concentration of glucose (5.00–200 μmol L−1). (d) The plot of I 440/I 570 versus the corresponding concentrations of glucose.

TEM images of HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. (a) CdTe QDs, (b) CDs, (c) CdTe QDs/CDs, (d) CDs+H2O2, (e) CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP, (f) CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP+H2O2. (CdTe QDs: 12 μmol L−1, CDs: 8 μmol L−1, HBcAb-HRP: 10 μL).

Visual detection of glucose in human serum by HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. All the images were taken upon excitation of 365 nm UV lamp. (CdTe QDs: 12 μmol L−1, CDs: 8 μmol L−1, HBcAb-HRP: 10 μL).
Analytical results of glucose in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Visual detection of H2O2 and glucose by HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the development of a sensitive and simple enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform in the consist of CdTe quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), and hepatitis B core antibody labeled with horseradish peroxidase (HBcAb-HRP) for the visual analysis of H2O2 and glucose. The sulfur atoms in HBcAb-HRP have a strong affinity for Cd(II), which effectively enhances the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs due to the generation of more radiative centers at the CdTe/Cd-SR complex. In the presence of H2O2, the Cd-S bonds are oxidized to form disulfide products and results in linear fluorescence quenching, while CDs maintain stable. Becasue glucose can be converted into H2O2 with the aid of glucose oxidase, this sensing platform can also be used for analyzing glucose. The detection limits for H2O2 and glucose are 2.9 μmol L−1 with RSD of 2.6% and 1.6 μmol L−1 with RSD of 2.4% respectively. In addition, under UV lamp irradiation, the orange-yellow CdTe QDs gradually quench with increasing H2O2 and glucose, while the blue CDs remain unchanged. A color change from orange-yellow to blue enables a visual semi-quantitative determination of H2O2 in commercial contact lens solution and glucose in human serum without any pretreatment. Thus, this CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric sensing platform has significant potential for the rapid analysis of H2O2 and glucose in actual application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advancement of social productivity, there has been a significant improvement in people's quality of life. However, there has also been a gradual increase in the prevalence of unhealthy lifestyle habits and dietary patterns. Consequently, obesity rates among residents have seen a sharp rise, accompanied by a year-on-year increase in the incidence of diabetes. By 2045, the number of diabetic patients is projected to reach 629 million [1]. Diabetes is primarily characterized by elevated blood glucose levels, making blood glucose measurement a crucial diagnostic criterion for this condition. Another important biological molecule, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) plays a pivotal role in maintaining physiological balance within organisms. Excessive H2O2 can bring serious damage to proteins and DNA, and results in various diseases, such as neurodegeneration, autoimmune diseases, and even cancer [2]. Therefore, the development of analytical method for detecting human blood glucose and H2O2 holds great significance.

Currently, there are several methods used for the detection of glucose and H2O2, including Rayleigh scattering spectroscopy [3], electrochemical methods [4], enzymatic sensors [5–7], colorimetric assays [8], and fluorescence sensors [9–12]. Among these methods, fluorescence methods have gained significant attention due to their low cost, simplicity, sensitivity, and selectivity. For instance, Cai et al. [13] synthesized blue fluorescent silicon nanodots in situ using aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and glucose for direct glucose detection. Ma et al. [14] developed an "on" fluorescent sensor for detecting glucose in serum by modifying carbon dots (CDs) with MnO2 nanosheets. However, it should be noted that these methods rely on the measurement of fluorescence intensity at a single wavelength, which can be prone to interference from external factors and complex matrix.

Ratiometric fluorescence sensors have gained considerable attention as they offer the advantage of self-calibration in addition to the potential for visual detection, effectively overcoming the limitations of single-wavelength fluorescence methods [15–18]. For instance, Cho et al. [16] developed CDs/rhodamine 6 G ratio fluorescence sensing platform for glucose detection, utilizing the internal standard signal of rhodamine 6 G. Glucose is converted into H2O2 through the catalytic action of GOx·H2O2 then facilitates the oxidation of terephthalic acid to terephthalate, which results in a new fluorescence emission peak. Building upon this principle, Wu et al. [17] proposed a method for the detection of glucose based on Au nanoparticle/terephthalic acid ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. Liu et al. [18] developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform using g-C3N4/OPD for glucose detection. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) is oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), producing the oxidized product 2,3-diaminophenazine that itself emits a new yellow fluorescence at 560 nm. It also stacks on g-C3N4 nanosheets through hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions, leading to the quenching of g-C3N4 fluorescence. However, the conventional methods used for synthesizing ratiometric fluorescence sensors tend to be complex and expensive. As a result, there is a pressing need to develop a glucose ratiometric fluorescence sensor that is simple, sensitive, and cost-effective.

In this work, an enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was developed by using a simple physical mixing approach of CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs), CDs, and horseradish peroxidase hepatitis core antibody (HBcAb-HRP). The platform was designed for visual detection of H2O2 and glucose, utilizing CdTe QDs as the detection signal and CDs as the internal standard signal. Upon UV lamp irradiation, the orange-yellow fluorescence emitted by CdTe QDs gradually diminished as the levels of H2O2 and glucose increased, while the blue fluorescence emitted by CDs remained unchanged. This change in fluorescence was accompanied by a visual transition from orange-yellow to blue fluorescence. Various working conditions were optimized, and the mechanisms for detecting H2O2 and glucose were investigated. Subsequently, these optimized conditions were applied to the detection of H2O2 and glucose in real samples such as lens care solutions, mineral water, and human serum. Furthermore, semi-quantitative visual analysis of H2O2 content in commercial lens care solutions and glucose content in human serum was attempted.
Detailed information was displayed in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of CdTe QDs and CDs was referred to the literatures [19,20]. The specific synthesis steps, determinations of content, and experimental procedures for quantum yield are all described in detail in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP for the detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform of CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP was prepared by physically mixing 1.2 × 10−5 mol L−1 CdTe QDs, 8 × 10−6 mol L−1 CDs, 10 μL HBcAb-HRP, and 12.5 mmol L−1 PBS (pH=7) at room temperature. The resulting mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 5 min

H2O2 standard solutions were sequentially added to the prepared sensing platform solution described above. Each solution was diluted to a final volume of 5 mL. After a reaction time of 20 min, the fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs (λem=570 nm) and CDs (λem=440 nm) in the mixture were recorded under excitation at 352 nm.

A series of glucose standard solutions were prepared by adding different concentrations of glucose to a solution containing 20 μg mL−1 GOx and the previously prepared sensing platform solution. Each solution was diluted to a final volume of 5 mL. The mixtures were then incubated for 60 min at 37 °C. After incubation, the fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs (λem=570 nm) and CDs (λem=440 nm) in the mixture were recorded under excitation at 352 nm.

Human serum samples were provided voluntarily from the Laboratory Department of the Second People's Hospital of Huangshi City. The collected samples were then centrifuged at 3500 rpm, and the resulting supernatant was carefully collected and stored at 4 ℃ for further use.
Lens care solution and mineral water samples were obtained from a local supermarket without any pre-treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of H2O2 and glucose in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: A series of H2O2 standard solutions with concentrations of 9.0 × 10−6, 2.0 × 10−5, 4.0 × 10−5, 6.0 × 10−5, 8.0 × 10−5, 1.0 × 10−4, 2.0 × 10−4, 3.0 × 10−4, 4.0 × 10−4 and 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 were prepared and added to the sensing platform solution, respectively. Each solution was diluted to a final volume of 5 mL. After incubating for 20 min, the resulting solutions were exposed to a UV lamp (λex=365 nm). The solutions were then photographed using a mobile phone. Similarly, a total of 1.0 mL of the commercially available contact lens solution and the spiked samples were separately mixed with the sensing platform solution. Each mixture was then diluted to a final volume of 5.0 mL and allowed to stand for 20 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixtures were exposed to a UV lamp (λex=365 nm). Finally, photographs of the mixtures were taken using a mobile phone.

A series of glucose standard solutions with concentrations of 5.0 × 10−6, 1.0 × 10−5, 3.0 × 10−5, 5.0 × 10−5, 7.0 × 10−5, 1.0 × 10−4, 1.5 × 10−4, 2 × 10−4 mol L−1 were prepared. Each standard solution was mixed with a solution containing 20 μg mL−1 GOx and the sensing platform solution. The mixtures were then diluted to a final volume of 5 mL. After incubation for 60 min at 37 °C, the resulting solutions were exposed to a UV lamp (λex=365 nm). Photographs of the solutions were taken using a mobile phone. Similarly, 50 μL of human serum and the spiked samples were mixed with a solution containing 20 μg mL−1 GOx and the sensing platform solution. The mixtures were then diluted to a final volume of 5 mL. After incubation for 60 min at 37 °C, the resulting solutions were exposed to a UV lamp (λex=365 nm). Photographs of the solutions were taken using a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principles for visual assay H2O2 and glucose by CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP
ce:section.ce:para: The graphical abstract illustrates the working principle of the enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform using CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP for the visual analysis of H2O2 and glucose. Sulfur donors [21,22] have been reported that can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs owing to promoting aggregation or generating more radiation centers through the formation of Cd-SR covalent complexes. Here, an enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform of CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP was developed by simply mixing physically CdTe QDs, CDs, and HBcAb-HRP. In the presence of H2O2, the Cd-S bonds at CdTe QDs are oxidized, resulting in the formation of organic sulfides (RS-SR). This leads to the detachment of HBcAb-HRP and thioglycolic acid from CdTe QDs, and the fluorescence of CdTe QDs become "turn-off". As a reference, the fluorescence of CDs remains unchanged. Furthermore, H2O2 is produced due to the reaction involving glucose and GOx. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the orange CdTe QDs can be also linearly quenched by the coexistence of GOx and glucose. When exposed to a UV lamp (λex=365 nm), the fluorescence intensity of the orange-yellow CdTe QDs gradually decreases with increasing H2O2 and glucose, but the fluorescence of the blue CDs remains unchanged. This change in fluorescence is accompanied by a visual transition from orange-yellow to blue. As a result, a visual semi-quantitative determination of H2O2 and glucose can be established using the enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform consisting of CdTe QDs/CDs/HBcAb-HRP.
Various techniques were employed to characterize the structure and properties of the enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform.

The FT-IR spectra of as-synthesized TGA-capped CdTe QDs, thioglycolic acid (TGA), and CDs are displayed in Fig. 1(a). It can be seen that the characteristic absorption band of CO vibration was shifted from 1700 cm−1 in TGA to1660 cm−1 in TGA-capped CdTe QDs. The stretching vibration peak of thiol at 2560 cm−1 was observed in TGA, but disappeared in CdTe QDs. These results strongly suggest that the thiol groups of TGA are linked to CdTe QDs surface through metal-sulfur bonding leaving the hydrophilic carboxylic groups directed outward. This makes the CdTe QDs surfaces negatively charged and become water-soluble as reported in the reference [22]. In the case of CDs, the FT-IR spectrum reveals several characteristic peaks. The stretching vibrations of -OH was observed in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1. The peaks at 2915, 1645, and 1384 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of -NH2, C-H, and the skeletal vibrations of the aromatic rings, respectively. These results indicate that the surface of CDs contains a substantial amount of amino and hydroxyl groups, which are consistent with the successful synthesis of CDs, as mentioned in references [23,24].

Fig. 1(b) and Fig. S1(a-c) present the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs, CDs, and HBcAb-HRP. In Fig. 1(b), CdTe QDs exhibit a broad absorption in the wavelength range of 270–400 nm, with a maximum UV absorption peak at 292 nm. Similarly, CDs also show a broad absorption band in the range of 300–400 nm, with a maximum absorption peak at 350 nm. HBcAb-HRP exhibits a narrow absorption peak at 287 nm. These absorption spectra indicate the distinct absorption characteristics of each component in the sensing platform. As shown in Fig. S1(a), the fluorescence emission spectrum of CdTe QDs has a wide excitation wavelength (300–500 nm) with a maximum emission wavelength at 570 nm. As shown in Fig. S1(b), the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are λex= 352 nm and λem= 440 nm, respectively. For HBcAb-HRP, the maximum emission wavelengths are λem= 282 nm and λem= 344 nm, as depicted in Fig. S1(c). These fluorescence spectra demonstrate that CdTe QDs and CDs can be effectively excited at a wavelength of 352 nm, while HBcAb-HRP cannot. The bandgap of CdTe has been carried out through the ultraviolet-visible transmission spectrum and the associated Tauc plot. As shown in Fig. S2, the bandgap of CdTe is 1.70 eV. In addition to simple synthesis, the prepared CDs in this work also exhibit excellent photostability and chemical stability. As shown in the Fig. S3 (a) and (b), after 12 h of continuous illumination and in the presence of up to 2 mol L−1 NaCl, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains stable. Therefore, CDs are well-suited as stable internal standards in ratiometric sensors.

Fig. 1(c) displays the Zeta potentials of CdTe QDs, CDs, CdTe QDs+CDs, CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP, and CdTe QDs+CDs+HBcAb-HRP. All of these Zeta potentials are negative, indicating that there is no interaction among the components due to electrostatic repulsion. Fig. 1(d) shows the fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs, CDs, HBcAb-HRP, CdTe QDs+CDs, CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP, CDs+HBcAb-HRP, and CdTe QDs+CDs+HBcAb-HRP under 352 nm excitation. It can be observed that CdTe QDs and CDs exhibit fluorescence, while HBcAb-HRP does not show any fluorescence under the same condition. Additionally, the fluorescence positions and intensities of CdTe QDs, CDs, and CdTe QDs/CDs remain consistent, indicating that they do not influence each other. However, upon the addition of HBcAb-HRP, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs increases at 570 nm, regardless of whether CdTe QDs were mixed with CDs or not. This enhancement is attributed to the generation of Cd-SR covalent complex between the sulfur in HBcAb-HRP and the Cd(II) in CdTe QDs. It leads to the aggregation of CdTe QDs and generates additional radiation centers, resulting in increased fluorescence intensity. Therefore, we describe it as enhanced ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, where "enhanced" refers to the fact that mixing CdTe QDs with HBcAb-HRP will enhance the fluorescence of CdTe QDs. We then investigated the chemical and optical stability of the sensing platform. As shown in Fig. S4, the results showed that the sensor signal remained almost unchanged within 6 h of illumination and 0.2 mol L−1 NaCl concentrations, indicating good optical and chemical stability. However, with further increases in time and salt content, the stability of CdTe QDs was affected, resulting in interference with the stability of the sensor's ratio signal.

Based on prior research conducted by our research group, it has been discovered that HBcAb-HRP can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and effectively improve the sensitivity of the method. In this particular ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, CdTe QDs play a pivotal role. This study capitalizes on this characteristic to enhance the sensitivity of the method. To investigate the effect of HBcAb-HRP amount on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs, experiments were conducted in a 12.5 mmol L−1 PBS solution with a pH of 7. The amount of HBcAb-HRP used ranged from 0 to 80 μL. As depicted in Fig. S5, it can be observed that as the amount of HBcAb-HRP increases, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs gradually enhances. When the volume of HBcAb-HRP is 10 μL, the maximum fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs is attained. Therefore, 10 μL of HBcAb-HRP was selected as the optimal condition.

To investigate the effect of different pH values on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs, CDs, and CdTe QDs/CDs, we conducted experiments with a fixed volume of 10 μL HBcAb-HRP. Fig. S6 illustrates the results obtained. From the data, it can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs is maximized at pH range from 7 to 10, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs hits its maximum value in the pH range of 6–11. Importantly, the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity remains consistent whether CdTe QDs and CDs are mixed or not. In strongly acidic environments, the protonation leads to quench the fluorescence of both CdTe QDs and CDs. Thus, we determined a pH of 7.0 in PBS as the optimal condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition of HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted experiments to optimize the composition of HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratio fluorescence sensing platform. Specifically, we fixed the amount of HBcAb-HRP (10 μL), the fluorescence spectra of 10 μmol L−1 CdTe QDs with the addition of CDs (4–12 μmol L−1), and the fluorescence spectra of 8 μmol L−1 CDs with CdTe QDs (8–16 μmol L−1) was investigated. The results, as displayed in Fig. S7, demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and CDs increases with increasing concentrations of their respective components. Importantly, the fluorescence intensity remains constant at a fixed concentration. To acquire a clear visual distinction, we selected 12 μmol L−1 CdTe QDs with orange fluorescence, 8 μmol L−1 CDs with blue fluorescence to construct HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratio fluorescence sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time on the detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S8 illustrates the impact of reaction time (ranging from 0 to 130 min) on the fluorescence intensity of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs for the detection of H2O2. The results reveal that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 570/I 440) gradually decreases as the reaction time increases from 0 to 20 min and the fluorescence intensity ratio remains relatively stable. Thus, a reaction time of 20 min was selected as the optimal condition for this ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GOx dosage and reaction time on the detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of CdTe QDs was kept at 1.2 × 10−5 mol L−1, CDs at 8 μmol L−1, HBcAb-HRP at 10 μL, and glucose at 12.5 mmol L−1 in PBS (pH=7). The influence GOx dosage ranging from 0 to 140 μg mL−1 on the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/I 570) of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was examined. As shown in Fig. S9, the results indicate that the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 440/I 570) gradually increases with increasing concentration of GOx. When the concentration of GOx is 20 μg mL−1, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 440/I 570) reaches its maximum and remains stable thereafter. Thus, 20 μg mL−1 of GOx was acted as the optimal condition for this ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform.

Fig. S10 illustrates the influence of reaction time (0–85 min) on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform in the existence of both glucose and GOx. The results indicate that during the initial 60 min, as the reaction time increases, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 570/I 440) gradually decreases. However, after 60 min, the ratio remains relatively stable and undergoes minimal changes. Thus, a reaction time of 60 min was the optimal condition for the detection of glucose.

We investigated the effects of various substances, including Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr2O7 2-, MnO4 -, ascorbic acid, tyrosine, aspartate, glycine, cystine, cysteine, and H2O2 on the performance of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. Each substance was evaluated separately. As depicted in Fig. S11(a), with the exception of H2O2, the presence of other substances had minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/I 570) of the CdTe QDs/CDs. It suggests that the sensor exhibits excellent selectivity towards H2O2. Furthermore, the effects of other glucose analogs, namely lactose, fructose, sucrose, and maltose on the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform containing 20 μg mL−1 GOx were evaluated under identical conditions. The results are displayed in Fig. S11(b) and apart from glucose, other glucose analogs showed minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/I 570) of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. It indicates that the proposed platform demonstrates excellent selectivity for glucose detection in the presence of GOx.

We assess the analytical performance of the developed HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs for the detection of H2O2 and glucose, and the results are shown in Fig. 2. As displayed in Fig. 2(a), the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs exhibits a linear decrease with an increase in H2O2 concentration ranging from 9.00 to 500 μmol L−1, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains almost unchanged. Fig. 2(b) depicts the calibration curve of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/I 570) versus H2O2 concentration for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. The linear equation for this relationship is I 440/I 570 = 1679.33 C + 0.56713, with an R2 value of 0.9971. The linear range extends from 9.00 to 500 μmol L−1, with an RSD of 2.59%. According to the IUPAC definition, the detection limit (LOD) is defined as 3 s/k, where s is the standard deviation of signal intensity obtained by nine replicate measurements for a blank sample, and k is the slope of the calibration equation. The LOD achieved is as low as 2.9 μmol L−1. Furthermore, as demonstrated in Fig. 2(c), the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs demonstrates a linear decrease with increasing glucose concentration (5.00 −200 μmol L−1), while the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains unaffected. Fig. 2(d) exhibits the calibration curve for the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 440/I 570) and glucose concentration in the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. The linear equation for this relationship is I 440/I 570 = 8557.78 C + 0.6904, with an R2 value of 0.9981. The linear range for glucose detection spans from 5.00 to 200 μmol L−1, with an RSD of 2.38%. The LOD is 1.6 μmol L−1.

In comparison to other detection methods, we evaluated the performance of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs for the detection of H2O2 and glucose. From Table S1, it can be observed that our method exhibits comparable detection levels to non-fluorescent methods. Although it is not as good as some fluorescence-based methods, our method possesses the capability of visualization and can also be utilized for glucose detection. Table S2 presents a comparison of the analytical performances of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs for glucose detection with other methods. In comparison to non-fluorescent methods, our method demonstrates improved sensitivity and simplicity of operation. Furthermore, when compared to other fluorescent sensing platforms, the LOD for glucose detection in this study falls within an intermediate range. Additionally, compared to single emission fluorescence methods, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform employed in our method effectively enables visual analysis and reduces the influence of measurement concentration fluctuations, false signals, and variations in excitation light intensity. As a result, it exhibits higher precision and accuracy in glucose detection, making it more suitable for practical applications in real-world scenarios.

The TEM photos of CdTe QDs/CDs are presented in Fig. 3. Specifically, Fig. 3(a-f) displays the TEM photos of CdTe QDs, CDs, CdTe QDs/CDs, CDs+H2O2, CdTe+HBcAb-HRP, and CdTe+HBcAb-HRP+H2O2. From Fig. 3(a-b), it is evident that both CdTe QDs and CDs exhibit monodisperse spherical shapes, with particle sizes of approximately 1.75 ± 0.38 nm and 1.78 ± 0.42 nm, respectively. Fig. 3(c) demonstrates that the CdTe QDs and CDs remain monodisperse and spherical after mixing, without any interference between them. The resulting particles have a size of approximately 1.78 ± 0.36 nm. Furthermore, based on Fig. 3(d), the morphology of CDs remains unchanged after the addition of H2O2, with a particle size of about 1.75 ± 0.23 nm. On the other hand, Fig. 3(e-f) reveals that the addition of HBcAb-HRP to CdTe QDs leads to aggregation and an increase in particle size to about 2.14 ± 0.48 nm. Subsequently, with the addition of H2O2, the surface of the aggregates becomes rough, resulting in a smaller particle size of approximately 1.80 ± 0.33 nm, along with the generation of bubbles. Based on our speculation, the mechanism for fluorescence quenching may involve the formation of a Cd-SR covalent complex, where the sulfur-containing donor atom in HBcAb-HRP has an affinity with Cd(II) in CdTe QDs. This complex promotes the aggregation of CdTe QDs and the formation of additional radiation centers, thus increasing the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs. The Cd-S bond at CdTe QDs is oxidized in the presence of H2O2, leading to the formation of organic sulfide (RS-SR). As a result, HBcAb-HRP and thioglycolic acid detach from CdTe QDs, which causes the fluorescence quenching of CdTe QDs.

As the CdTe QDs play a key role of the indicator in the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, we further investigated the difference in fluorescence lifetime of CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP before and after mixing with H2O2. The corresponding results are presented in Fig. S12. We observed that there were no significant alterations in the fluorescence lifetime of CdTe QDs (τ0 =29.385 ns, τ1 =28.567 ns) and CdTe QDs+HBcAb-HRP (τ0 ´=31.229 ns, τ1 ´=30.607 ns). This finding indicates that the quenching process is primarily governed by static quenching, rather than dynamic quenching.

To evaluate the feasibility and practicality of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for H2O2 detection, we use it to measure H2O2 in human serum, lens care solution, and mineral water under optimal conditions. The obtained results are summarized in Table S3. We successfully measured the concentrations of H2O2 in the lens care solution (34.88 μmol L−1) and mineral water (9.27 μmol L−1). The recoveries obtained in these samples ranged from 95.6% to 106.5%, indicating good accuracy. Additionally, RSDs were less than 5% (n = 5), suggesting high precision in the analysis. These results demonstrate that this ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform has excellent potential for detecting H2O2 in various matrix samples.

To further assess the practical application of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, we conducted experiment to measure the glucose content in human serum. 50 μL human serum was added to the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/ CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. The results are presented in Table 1 and we detected glucose concentrations of 6.07, 4.33, and 4.15 mmol L−1 in the human serum samples that fall well within the normal range of glucose concentrations found in the human body (3.9–6.1 mmol L−1). Additionally, the recovery rate of the standard addition tests ranged between 95.3% and 110.6%. It indicates the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs holds good application in accurately determining glucose levels in human serum.

In Fig. 4, the upper row displays images of the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform after interacting with glucose standard solutions of various concentrations (0–200 μmol L−1). The lower row presents images of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform after interacting with different human serum. As depicted in the Fig. 4, the color of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform transitions from orange to blue under UV light as the glucose concentration increases. By comparing the color changes in the upper and lower rows, we have successfully developed a novel method that utilizes the HBcAb-HRP fluorescence-enhanced CdTe QDs/CDs for visual detection of human blood glucose. The developed method enables the semi-quantitative detection of glucose in human serum within the range of 0–200 μmol L−1.

In this study, we successfully constructed a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform by physically mixing CdTe QDs, CDs and HBcAb-HRP. The addition of HBcAb-HRP led to the aggregation of CdTe QDs, resulting in enhancing the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs. After the addition of H2O2, the Cd-S bond in CdTe QDs was oxidized and it quenches the fluorescence of CdTe QDs while the fluorescence of CDs remained unchanged. This strategy was successfully used for the detection of H2O2 in human serum, lens care solutions, and mineral water. Furthermore, we exploited the catalytic capability of GOx to generate H2O2 from glucose, enabling the detection of glucose in human serum. We devised a novel method for the visual semi-quantitative detection of glucose. The observed color change from orange to blue in the fluorescent sensing platform solution with increasing glucose concentration under UV light. Our method offers several advantages, including simplicity of operation, low cost, high sensitivity, and visual detection. It holds significant potential for applications in human blood glucose monitoring and diabetes diagnosis. Moreover, based on the underlying detection principle, we believe that this method holds strong potential for application in fields such as biomedicine and furniture cleaning, where the generation of H2O2 is involved.
This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China. [2022CFD038].

Additional information about reagents and instruments, preparation of CdTe QDs and CDs, detection of quantum yield, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CdTe QDs, CDs and HBcAb-HRP (Fig. S1), Tauc plot of CdTe QDs (Fig. S2), photostability and chemical stability of CDs (Fig. S3), CdTe QDs and ratiometric sensor (Fig. S4), effect of HBcAb-HRP concentration on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs (Fig. S5), effects of pH (Fig. S6), composition (Fig. S7) and reaction time (Fig. S8) on the ratiometric sensor for detecting H2O2, effects of GOx concentration (Fig. S9) and response time (Fig. S10) on ratiometric sensor for detecting glucose, selectivity of the ratiometric sensor for H2O2 detection and glucose detection (Fig. S11), fluorescence life time decay curves (Fig. S12), comparison of the method with other methods for H2O2 detection (Table S1) and glucose detection (Table S2), and analytical results of H2O2 in actual samples (Table S3) as noted in text.

Cao Jie: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jiang Han: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wu Yiwei: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yu Xiaoxiao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
